segments.text: Okay, Harris, you have been in the US for a few days already.
How are you feeling in training as you get ready for the Club World Cup?
Yeah, it's been good.
Obviously, you know, it's a bit of a journey to get here from Auckland.
So, you know, the boys have been, you know, putting in a shift in training.
We've been working on a lot of things that we're going to implement in the games which are coming up.
And it's been really good.
The spirits are high and the boys are buzzing.
And, yeah, we're also grateful to be here on such a big occasion.
So, yeah, the spirits are very high.
This tournament will be special for you as well because you will be the first player from Pakistan
participating in this competition.
What do you feel about it?
Yeah, honestly, it's just such an honour and a blessing.
You know, it's hard to really put into words.
But to be honest, when I was a bit young, I never really thought, you know,
something like this would be achievable.
But for me to be on the world stage and representing Auckland City, New Zealand,
but then also Pakistan, you know, that's a dream country, you know,
I just hope that I can inspire a lot of young kids around the world
and put Pakistan on the map as well.
Do you get a lot of messages from them?
Yeah, to be honest, one thing with the Pakistani fans and just the community
is that they're super supportive, you know,
since I've been playing in New Zealand at the top level, you know,
like I check my Instagram messages and all that, you know,
and they're really, really supportive.
Even when I signed for Auckland City, it went, you know, all over the news in Pakistan,
which was so good and really made my parents happy, you know,
which is so important to me.
And then also even the Pakistani community that I have back in Christchurch, you know,
for them it's such like a, it's an honour because I grew up with them
and then to have someone from the community to play on the world stage, you know,
it's such a blessing.
So, yeah, now they've been really supportive.
Everyone around the world from Pakistan has been amazing.
I have the feeling that must be great to have all that support,
but probably it's a lot of pressure as well.
So what helps you to keep calm in this kind of context like music, religion?
What helps you the most?
So when I was coming from a Pakistani background, like I'm a very strong Muslim.
And so for me, I understand that, you know, these tournaments are so good
and such a big occasion, but I find a lot of peace with God himself.
And so for me, as a Muslim, we pray five times a day.
So that's five times during the day where, you know,
I'm getting myself out of, you know, this bubble that I'm in with the football
and focusing on my religion with God.
And obviously I think that helps me a lot, be able to kind of stay calm, I guess, you know.
That kind of helps my nerves and lets me, you know, play the best I can.
And last one, if you could dream big in this tournament,
what would be the best thing for you in the Club World Cup?
Yeah, the best thing definitely would probably be to get a win to us
or even a draw if we can get through Bayern Munich, Benfica or Boca and Manisa,
you know, get three points, that'd be such a blessing.
But then also being an attacker, you know, if I was to manage to get a goal, you know,
and help the team win, that'd be the cherry on top, you know.
So we'll see what happens, but yeah, that would definitely be such a good moment.
